title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 13, 2026
headline: Tunisia's proposed score cut is reaffirmed for a fourth day as the published score holds at 34.4.
summary: Tunisia's proposed score cut, from 34.4 to 23.8 out of 100, was reaffirmed for a fourth straight day. No new escalation emerged beyond the mass sentencing of 21 opposition figures already behind the proposal, so the published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. Iran's own conduct -- political executions at a multi-decade high -- intensifies an already-documented pattern, but Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 sits so close to zero that even a move to the floor falls short of the five-point shift needed to change a published score. Separately, the United States struck Iranian military targets again on July 12 and President Trump threatened to reinstate a blockade of Iranian oil shipments on July 13; that action is scored against the United States, not Iran. Venezuela's earthquake death toll climbed to 4,490, twenty days after the quake, but the increase reflects the disaster's scale rather than a new government failure, so its score holds at 18 of 100. Israel, Sudan, Russia, and Yemen all remain at the lowest possible score, 0 of 100, as fresh evidence of harm continues to arrive in each country. Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100 with no new trigger this cycle, after the China backdoor allegation was already tested and held on July 12. A long-running question about whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for similar conduct is now open about twenty days.

pipeline.tunisiaHandling: Tunisia's proposed score of 23.8 of 100 (down from a published 34.4) remains a proposal only, reaffirmed for a fourth cycle with no new dated escalation beyond the already-reflected mass-sentencing record. The published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. No change has been made to the live index.

topSignals.title: Tunisia's Proposed Score Cut Is Reaffirmed for a Fourth Day With No New Escalation
topSignals.whyItMatters: A proposed score cut has now sat unresolved for four nights running with fully consistent evidence, but the published score does not move until the benchmark's editors act on it.
topSignals.description: Tunisia holds a published score of 34.4 out of 100, in the Developing band. A review proposed on July 10 suggested a lower score of 23.8 out of 100, based on the 25-year prison sentence given to Sihem Bensedrine, the former head of Tunisia's own Truth and Dignity Commission, and the mass sentencing of 21 opposition figures to 12-to-35-year prison terms on July 8. Tonight, no new dated escalation surfaced beyond that already-reflected record. A fresh Human Rights Watch article, published July 7, restates the same crackdown pattern rather than adding new evidence. The proposed figure of 23.8 is unchanged, and no second, duplicate proposal was opened. The published score of 34.4 stays in place while the benchmark's editors review the proposal for a fourth consecutive night. Confidence: medium.

evidence.quote: Former truth commission president Sihem Bensedrine sentenced to 25 years in prison
evidence.claim: Tunisia is imprisoning the leader of its own past accountability body, reversing transitional justice.

evidence.quote: Tunisia: Harsh Sentences for Rights Defenders
evidence.claim: A fresh Human Rights Watch piece restates the existing crackdown pattern rather than adding a new dated escalation.

topSignals.title: Iran's Near-Zero Score Cannot Fall Further as the US-Iran Ceasefire Keeps Collapsing
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran's own record of executions is intensifying, but the score is already so close to zero that even severe new evidence cannot register as a change under the benchmark's own math.
topSignals.description: Iran holds a published score of 2.5 out of 100, in the Critical band. Political executions and repression continue at a multi-decade high, deepening a pattern already counted against Iran's score. But 2.5 points is nearly the entire distance left to the lowest possible score, so even a move all the way to zero falls short of the five-point shift the benchmark requires before changing a published number. Separately, the ceasefire between the United States and Iran kept collapsing this week: the US military struck Iranian targets near the Strait of Hormuz again on July 12, using one-way attack drones for the first time in this campaign, and President Trump said on July 13 that the US would reinstate a blockade of Iranian oil shipments. That action is scored against the United States' own conduct, not against Iran, and the United States was not among tonight's fifteen assessed countries. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: The US completed a second round of strikes on Iranian targets near the Strait of Hormuz, and CENTCOM said it used one-way attack aerial and sea drones for the first time in this campaign; President Trump said the US would reinstate a blockade of Iranian oil-shipping vessels.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake Death Toll Passes 4,490 Twenty Days In, Still Short of the Score-Change Line
topSignals.whyItMatters: A death toll that keeps rising from a natural disaster is not, by itself, evidence of government misconduct, so Venezuela's score stays the same even as the number climbs.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds a published score of 18 out of 100, in the Critical band. Twenty days after the earthquake, the government's official toll reached 4,490 dead as of July 13, about 150 more than two days earlier, with 16,740 injured and more than 19,500 people now living in displacement camps. Most of that increase reflects continued recovery of bodies from collapsed buildings, which the benchmark treats as a consequence of the disaster's scale, not new evidence of government failure. This window's actual government action was a modest positive: new housing-plot allocations and roughly 100,000 aid kits distributed with US support. The evidenced decline in Venezuela's own-conduct response score stays in the low negative 3-point range, short of the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Venezuela's official earthquake death toll reached 4,490 as of July 13, roughly 150 higher than the 4,333 reported two days earlier, with 16,740 injured and no estimate given for the number still unaccounted for.

topSignals.title: Israel, Sudan, Russia, and Yemen All Hold at Zero as Fresh Harm Keeps Arriving
topSignals.whyItMatters: All four countries already sit at the lowest possible score. Tonight's evidence cannot lower any of them further, but each event reinforces why the score sits at the floor.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100. An Israeli strike on Gaza's Sabra neighborhood killed 8 Palestinians, including a 9-year-old girl, on July 13; separate strikes and gunfire killed more, and the cumulative toll since the ceasefire began has passed 1,100 killed. Sudan also holds at 0 of 100. New reporting quantified the Rapid Support Forces' drone campaign against El Obeid for the first time: 15 strikes over three weeks killed at least 45 civilians, and the European Parliament called on the EU to designate the group a terrorist organization. Russia holds at 0 of 100, where the same missile and drone campaign against Ukraine remains the operative record, and the European Union sanctioned six individuals over chemical-weapons development tied to the 2024 death of opposition figure Alexei Navalny. Yemen holds at 0 of 100, where the war's largest planned prisoner exchange, involving roughly 1,700 detainees, remains collapsed with no new implementation date set. None of the four scores can move lower, since all are already at the floor. Confidence: high for all four.

evidence.claim: An Israeli strike on the Sabra neighborhood south of Gaza City killed 8 Palestinians, including a 9-year-old girl, on July 13; the cumulative confirmed toll since the ceasefire began on October 10, 2025 now exceeds 1,100 killed and 3,500 injured.

Fifteen drone strikes over three weeks killed at least 45 civilians in El Obeid, repeatedly hitting markets, schools, fuel stations, and water infrastructure amid a siege of roughly 500,000 trapped civilians.

topSignals.title: The Mali-Burkina Faso Scoring Question Reaches Roughly Twenty Days Open
topSignals.whyItMatters: A long-running question about whether two neighboring countries are scored consistently for similar conduct still has no answer, now nearing three weeks unresolved.
topSignals.description: Mali holds at 12.5 out of 100, in the Critical band. Tonight, Mali confirmed again as a fuel and food blockade by the armed group JNIM continued to cripple the capital, Bamako, and the government said it broke a separate rebel blockade around a northern town. The dominant source of harm remains a non-state armed group, not the government's own conduct, so Mali's score does not move. The open question is different: whether Mali's score of 12.5 is fairly calibrated against Burkina Faso's 6.3, given that both governments face comparable armed-group violence and comparable documented abuses by their own forces. That question has now gone without a decision for roughly twenty consecutive days, the benchmark's second-longest open item after Tunisia's pending proposal, and it remains a matter for the benchmark's coordinating team rather than a single night's assessment. Burkina Faso itself was not assessed tonight. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Mali's military said it broke a rebel blockade around a strategic northern town, following a Thursday-night attack by an allied Tuareg armed group on an army reinforcement convoy.

topSignals.title: Anthropic Holds at 59.1 With No New Trigger as the China Backdoor Allegation Stays Held
topSignals.whyItMatters: A rival government's allegation from earlier this week still does not clear the benchmark's bar for a score change, and no new development arrived tonight to reopen the question.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds a published score of 59.1 out of 100, in the Functional band. The Chinese government's allegation that Claude Code contains a hidden backdoor -- tested against the benchmark's conversion-trigger rule and held on July 12 -- remains the operative record; no new independently-corroborated episode arrived this cycle. Separately, Alibaba instructed its own employees to stop using Anthropic's tools starting July 10, which the benchmark reads as a competitive and geopolitical response, not a new safety or disclosure finding. The published score holds at 59.1, with the heightened downward watch set on July 12 remaining in place. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Anthropic continues to reject China's backdoor allegation, characterizing the mechanism as an anti-distillation experiment already removed in a July 1 release, before the Chinese alert surfaced.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. The Ethnic Unity Law and Hong Kong prison-rules tightening continue, and the territory's last active pro-democracy party disbanded this window; no newly and reliably dated escalation surfaced beyond the already-confirmed record.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. No new conversion-trigger event arrived tonight; Alibaba's internal ban on employee use of Anthropic tools is read as a competitive and geopolitical response, not a new safety finding. The heightened watch set July 12 remains in place.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Fresh Israeli drone strikes on July 10 remain the dominant source of harm this window, not Lebanon's own conduct; an unconfirmed report that Israel's military was ordered to avoid 'sensitive operations' in the south does not yet offset the sustained strike pattern.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. A Balochistan security operation now reports a cumulative 117 alleged militants killed as of July 13, atop the already-priced enforced-disappearance pattern; the figure is security-source-reported and not independently verified.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. The earthquake death toll reached 4,490 twenty days in, but the increase is attributed to disaster scale, not new government conduct; the evidenced decline remains in the low negative 3-point range, short of the 5-point threshold.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. The World Food Programme warned of imminent food-assistance cuts as violence and hunger surge across the north, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. The Ebola outbreak crossed 600 deaths, the fastest-growing on the continent per Africa CDC, and healthcare workers in Ituri province walked off the job over unpaid wages, threatening response capacity.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. A fuel and food blockade by the armed group JNIM continues to cripple Bamako. The calibration question against Burkina Faso has now been open roughly twenty consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight. The calibration question against Mali remains open roughly twenty consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Bolivia's conduct profile, as an elected government under economic and civil-unrest strain, against Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity is the next event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Burkina Faso and Somalia remains open.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: Tunisia's pending score-review proposal has now gone four full cycles without a decision from the benchmark's editors, even as the underlying evidence stays fully consistent.
emergingRisks.detail: The proposed 23.8 of 100 stays within the Developing band and is not yet applied to the published record. A fourth consecutive cycle without action keeps the published score at a value the evidence no longer fully supports.

emergingRisks.risk: Renewed US strikes on Iran and a threatened Hormuz blockade are accumulating as United States conduct that is not yet reflected in any country's own score this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: The United States' Critical-band composite (17.5) was not reassessed tonight even as its interstate conduct toward Iran escalated with a first-ever use of one-way attack drones and a blockade threat.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake-response score has compounded across a fourth consecutive cycle without crossing the 5-point change threshold.
emergingRisks.detail: The evidenced own-conduct decline has deepened toward the low negative 3-point range twenty days after the quake. A discrete own-conduct failure, not further toll growth, is the trigger that would move this to a scored change.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring-consistency question is now open roughly twenty consecutive days, the benchmark's second-longest unresolved item after Tunisia.
emergingRisks.detail: Mali confirmed again tonight with a non-state armed group as the dominant source of harm, leaving the underlying calibration question about the two countries' government-conduct scores unresolved.

emergingRisks.risk: A newly-surfaced Ituri healthcare-worker wage dispute is compounding the Democratic Republic of the Congo's Ebola response, though the near-floor score cannot register it as a change yet.
emergingRisks.detail: Healthcare workers in the hardest-hit province walked off the job over unpaid wages, layering a new own-conduct-adjacent factor atop the already-priced army-redeployment and blocked-access record.

emergingRisks.risk: A cluster of near-zero scores across six countries means severe new evidence in any of them can no longer register as a public score change.
emergingRisks.detail: Six of tonight's fifteen assessed entities are at or within roughly 2.5 points of the absolute floor, illustrating how the benchmark's near-floor arithmetic can outpace even severe escalations.

No duplicate proposal was opened; the reaffirmation log was updated for a fourth consecutive night.

Separately, the US struck Iranian targets again on July 12 and threatened a Hormuz blockade on July 13 -- interstate conduct scored against the United States, not Iran.

The evidenced own-conduct decline stays in the low negative 3-point range, short of the 5-point change threshold.

Pakistan (17.2 of 100): a Balochistan security operation now reports 117 alleged militants killed since July 5, atop the already-priced enforced-disappearance pattern; the figure remains security-source-reported and unverified.

sectorAlerts.sector: AI Labs -- A Held Allegation Stays Held With No New Trigger

Alibaba's internal ban on employee use of Anthropic's tools is read as a competitive and geopolitical response, not a new safety finding.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2, the next forward date for this entity's boundary-watch status.

methodologyNotes.name: A Pending Proposal Can Be Reaffirmed Without Changing Its Number
methodologyNotes.description: When the evidence behind a score-change proposal stays consistent night after night, the benchmark records that the finding was reaffirmed rather than opening a new, overlapping proposal. Tonight, no new escalation surfaced beyond Tunisia's already-reflected mass-sentencing record for a fourth consecutive cycle. The proposed score of 23.8 out of 100 stayed the same; the published score remains 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score Near Zero Has Little Room Left to Fall
methodologyNotes.description: Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 is so close to the lowest possible mark that even a severe increase in documented executions cannot produce the 5-point change the benchmark requires before adjusting a published score. The Democratic Republic of the Congo (2.3) sits in the same position. The benchmark records these as near-floor confirmations rather than treating the arithmetic limit as evidence that the underlying conduct does not matter.

methodologyNotes.name: Interstate Harm Is Scored Against the Actor, Not the Target
methodologyNotes.description: When one country's military strikes another country, the benchmark scores that action against the acting country's own conduct, not against the country that was struck. Tonight, renewed US strikes on Iranian targets and a threatened blockade of Iranian oil shipments are logged as United States conduct, not scored against Iran's own record.

methodologyNotes.name: A Natural Disaster's Rising Toll Is Not, by Itself, New Misconduct
methodologyNotes.description: When a death toll from an earthquake, flood, or similar disaster keeps climbing, the benchmark separates deaths caused by the disaster's scale from deaths caused by a government's own failure to respond. Venezuela's earthquake toll passed 4,490 twenty days after the quake, but most of the increase reflects continued recovery of bodies from collapsed buildings, not a new government failure, so the score held.

forwardTriggers.trigger: The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. A second undisclosed telemetry episode or a regulator finding of deceptive practice would be the next event that could move the score.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.

The benchmark's editors act on the proposed 23.8 of 100 downgrade, now reaffirmed for a fourth cycle. Release of detained rights defenders or reinstatement of the shuttered information-access authority would be conditions for an upgrade instead.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Roughly twenty days open with no set review date.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
Coordinator-level review of whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions. No review date has been set.
A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.

A future priority-cluster assessment of the United States' own conduct toward Iran, including the renewed strikes and threatened Hormuz blockade, is the next event that could move the United States' Critical-band composite.
First confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.

Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.
Independent verification of any renewed attempt at the collapsed prisoner exchange is the next event that would allow a scored assessment.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Tonight's Iran assessment logs renewed US strikes and a threatened Hormuz blockade under United States conduct rather than Iran's composite; does that interstate harm eventually resolve into Iran's own near-floor score, or does the attribution-split rule hold it exclusively against the United States' Critical-band composite (17.5)?
